flashcards.front: What is memory management in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Memory management is the OS function that controls and coordinates computer memory, allocating portions to processes and ensuring efficient utilization of primary memory (RAM) while maintaining performance and protection.

flashcards.front: What is swapping in memory management?
flashcards.back: Swapping is the process of moving entire processes between main memory and secondary storage (disk), allowing more processes to run than can fit in physical memory.

Contiguous memory allocation assigns each process a single continuous block of memory. The entire process must fit in one block, leading to issues like external fragmentation and difficulty in memory expansion.

flashcards.front: What is paging in memory management?
flashcards.back: Paging divides both physical memory and logical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames and pages respectively. It eliminates external fragmentation but may cause internal fragmentation.

flashcards.front: What is segmentation in memory management?
flashcards.back: Segmentation divides memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions of a program (code, data, stack). Each segment has a name and length, providing logical view of memory.

Virtual memory is a technique that allows execution of processes that may not fit entirely in physical memory by using disk space as an extension of RAM, enabling larger programs to run on systems with limited memory.

flashcards.front: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: TLB is a special cache that stores recent translations of virtual memory addresses to physical addresses, speeding up the address translation process in paging systems.
Thrashing occurs when the OS spends more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing processes, caused by excessive page faults when processes do not have enough allocated frames.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation?
flashcards.back: Internal fragmentation occurs when allocated memory is larger than requested (unused space inside allocated block). External fragmentation occurs when free memory is divided into small non-contiguous blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the Working Set model?
flashcards.back: The Working Set model defines the set of pages a process is currently using (in its working set). The OS ensures enough frames are allocated to keep this set in memory to minimize page faults.

flashcards.front: What is a page replacement algorithm?
flashcards.back: A page replacement algorithm decides which page to evict from memory when a new page needs to be loaded and all frames are full. Common algorithms include FIFO, LRU, and Optimal.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the Memory Management Unit (MMU)?
flashcards.back: The MMU is hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses. It uses page tables to perform this translation quickly and provides memory protection between processes.

flashcards.front: What is compaction in memory management?
flashcards.back: Compaction (or garbage collection) is the process of moving all free memory into one contiguous block by moving allocated blocks together, eliminating external fragmentation.
Demand paging loads pages into memory only when they are referenced (on-demand), rather than loading the entire process at startup. This reduces I/O and memory usage for large programs.
A page fault occurs when a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory. The OS must then retrieve the page from secondary storage (disk), causing a delay.
